coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel g-C3N4/β-FeOOH Z-Scheme heterostructure photocatalyst modified with carbon quantum dots for efficient degradation of RhB
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Juhua
Liu, Xing
Gu, Jieliang
Zhao, Wenjie
Gu, Minming
Xie, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The degradation of organic pollutants using semiconductor photocatalysts is a new ecological approach, but the currently available photocatalysts are not very efficient. Herein, in order to obtain efficient visible-light photocatalysts, g-C3N4/β-FeOOH-modified carbon quantum dots (CDs) composite photocatalysts with Z-Scheme charge transfer mechanism were successfully synthesized. The phase composition and morphology of the composite were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques. Due to the upconversion effect of the CDs, the optical response range of the composite was effectively widened, and the optical utilization rate was improved. The Z-Scheme heterostructure not only improves the light trapping ability, significantly inhibits charge-carrier complexation, and realizes the spatial separation of redox sites, but also ensures that the photocatalyst maintains a suitable valence-conductivity band position and maintains the strong redox reactivity. In addition, CDs have the unique characteristics of electronic storage and transfer, which effectively enhance the quantum separation efficiency of the composite. The photocatalytic efficiency was measured by degrading rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light. The degradation performance was the best when the weight ratio of CDs was 6%, and the RhB solution degradation rate reached 100 % in 60 min. The unique structure and reliable mechanism provide a way for the development of advanced photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Degradation
Visible light
g-C3N4
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation process, structural and morphological analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental flowchart for preparing the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite.
(a) XRD patterns of g-C3N4, CF, CCF1, CCF2, and CCF3, (b) FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4, β-FeOOH, and CCF2.
XPS spectra of CCF2: (a) survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) Fe 2p.
(a) SEM, (b–d) HRTEM, and (e, f) mapping images of CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB with the as-prepared photocatalysts under visible light, (b) the corresponding pseudofirst-order kinetic plots, (c) cycling runs of CCF2 for RhB degradation under visible light, and (d) XRD patterns of CCF2 before and after photocatalysis.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-obtained samples. The plots of (αhν)2 vs. photon energy (hν) of (b) g-C3N4, (c) g-C3N4/β-FeOOH, and (d) CCF2.
(a) Transient photocurrents response of g-C3N4, CF, and CCF2 under visible light irradiation, (b) PL spectra of CF, CCF1, CCF2, and CCF3.
Photocatalytic degradation curves of CCF2 with different radical scavengers.
Schematic diagram of the proposed reaction mechanism over CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH under visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Construction of novel g-C3N4/β-FeOOH Z-Scheme heterostructure photocatalyst modified with carbon quantum dots for efficient degradation of RhB

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Eco-Environmental Materials, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Eco-Environmental Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bJiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Eco-Environmental Materials, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The degradation of organic pollutants using semiconductor photocatalysts is a new ecological approach, but the currently available photocatalysts are not very efficient. Herein, in order to obtain efficient visible-light photocatalysts, g-C3N4/β-FeOOH-modified carbon quantum dots (CDs) composite photocatalysts with Z-Scheme charge transfer mechanism were successfully synthesized. The phase composition and morphology of the composite were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques. Due to the upconversion effect of the CDs, the optical response range of the composite was effectively widened, and the optical utilization rate was improved. The Z-Scheme heterostructure not only improves the light trapping ability, significantly inhibits charge-carrier complexation, and realizes the spatial separation of redox sites, but also ensures that the photocatalyst maintains a suitable valence-conductivity band position and maintains the strong redox reactivity. In addition, CDs have the unique characteristics of electronic storage and transfer, which effectively enhance the quantum separation efficiency of the composite. The photocatalytic efficiency was measured by degrading rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light. The degradation performance was the best when the weight ratio of CDs was 6%, and the RhB solution degradation rate reached 100 % in 60 min. The unique structure and reliable mechanism provide a way for the development of advanced photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by organic dyes has become one of the most urgent environmental pollution problems in the world. The photocatalytic degradation method has the advantages of high efficiency, environmental friendliness, and wide applicability in the treatment of these organic pollutants. Semiconductor materials are currently the most stable and widely used catalysts and can be divided into the following categories: metal oxides [1–3], multicomponent oxides [4–8], metallic sulfides [9–11], bismuth oxides [12,13], layered metal oxides [14], silver compounds [15–17], and organic semiconductor materials [18,19].

As a new nonmetallic semiconductor photocatalytic material, g-C3N4 is responsive to visible light and has excellent acid, alkali, and light corrosion resistances, which has attracted increasing attention from researchers. For instance, Dong et al. [20] prepared the soft-templated graded porous g-C3N4 with uniform organic microstructure and exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity and stability. Su et al. [21] designed the Cu-coordinated mesoporous g-C3N4 nanotubes composites, and the photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of this catalyst under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) was 6.53 mmol g–1 h–1, which was much higher than the bulk phase g-C3N4 (0.58 mmol g–1 h–1). Both of them exhibited relatively excellent photocatalytic performance. However, the low specific surface area, low quantum efficiency, and poor adsorption capacity of g-C3N4 limit its application. Thereby, many scholars have used some methods including synthesis [22], surface modification [23], and elemental doping [24] to modify g-C3N4 to enhance its photocatalytic activity.

Metal hydroxide nanomaterials are widely used in energy and environmental fields due to their excellent catalytic properties. Specifically, β-FeOOH, whose band gap of β-FeOOH is approximately 2.12 eV, has catalytic capacity under visible light. In addition, it can be easily obtained in various forms without the use of surfactants [25–27]. For example, Akharame et al. [28] compounded g-C3N4 with β-FeOOH and significantly improved the photo-absorption capacity of the catalyst. The degradation rate of g-C3N4/β-FeOOH reached 93% within 120 min. Wang et al. [29] used in situ stable growth of g-C3N4 by β-FeOOH on g-C3N4 for deep oxidation of emerging pollutants by photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate under solar radiation, which showed excellent stability and reusability. However, the material's utilization of visible light was still not enough and the quantum yield was low, hindering the catalytic performance. How to further enhance its catalytic activity still needs to be explored.

As a new catalyst research hotspot, semiconductor quantum dots with unique up-conversion luminescence properties can be hybridized with inorganic, organic, polymer, and biological materials [30]. However, metallic quantum dots are usually unstable and highly toxic, so their applications are greatly restricted. In contrast, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have the advantages of nontoxicity, low preparation cost, environmental friendliness, and so on. In addition, due to their excellent fluorescence properties and electronic properties, CDs have attracted great attention in the field of photocatalysis [31–33]. Notably, the rational design and construction of Z-Scheme heterostructures are also one of the important ways to enhance photocatalytic activity. Z-Scheme heterostructure can not only improve the light trapping ability, significantly inhibit charge-carrier complexation, and realize the spatial separation of redox sites, but also ensure that the photocatalysts maintain the appropriate valence-conductivity band positions, keep the strong reduction/oxidation ability of photoexcited electrons/holes, promote surface/interface catalytic reactions, and maintain a strong redox reaction ability [34].

Herein, CDs were fabricated by hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source and compounded with g-C3N4/β-FeOOH to form the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH Z-Scheme heterostructure composite. In this structure, both 1D β-FeOOH nanorods and CDs were loaded on 2D g-C3N4 nanosheets, which contributed to the fast interfacial charge transfer. Subsequently, the photocatalytic properties of the composites were investigated by measuring the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light. The results showed that when the weight ratio of CDs was 6%, it was able to completely degrade 50 mL of 10 mg L–1 RhB solution within 60 min with an apparent rate constant of 0.057 min–1. Meanwhile, the effect of CDs on the photocatalytic activity and the composite material was explored by photoelectrochemical characterization, band gap, and conductance band tests. The photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the composites was investigated. In addition, the chemical stability of the samples was tested by cycling experiments. This work will provide excellent guidance for the future synthesis of more efficient, green and sustainable, and stable semiconductor photocatalysts.

Urea (21 g) was dissolved in 28 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to a crucible and put into a muffle furnace. The temperature of the muffle furnace was first raised to 400 °C at a rate of 15 °C min–1, held for 1 h, and then raised to 500 °C at the same rate and held for 2 h. After the furnace naturally cooled, the sample was collected and ground to obtain yellow g-C3N4 powder.

CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. 3 g of urea and 15 g of citric acid were dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL reactor, and the temperature was increased to 180 °C and held for 5 h. After the reactor naturally cooled, the solution was centrifuged and dialyzed. Finally, the dialyzed external fluid was freeze-dried at –40 °C for 48 h to obtain CD solids. The CD powder was refrigerated at 4 °C.

0.6 g of g-C3N4 and 0.03 g of CDs were dispersed in 30 mL of distilled water by ultrasound and stirred to form solution A. Then, 1.215 g of FeCl3·6H2O was added to 30 mL of distilled water to form solution B. Solution B was mixed with solution A and stirred for 1 h. Afterward, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL reactor and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After that, the solution was filtered, and the precipitate was dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. Under the same experimental conditions, the samples were prepared by adjusting the content of CDs (3 wt%, 6 wt%, and 9 wt%) and labelled CCF1, CCF2, and CCF3. For comparison, g-C3N4/β-FeOOH was acquired with the same method without CDs, which was labelled CF.

The phase composition and structure of the samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical, X'pert3 Powder) with Cu Kα radiation. The microstructure was observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI, Nova NanoSEM 450) and a field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JLM-2100). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out using an infrared spectrophotometer (NICOLET, NEXUS-670) in the range of 4000 to 400 cm–1. The surface chemical state of the samples was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi). Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis) of the samples were obtained using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600 plus) with measured wavelengths ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) was detected using a fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO, FP-6500). The standard excitation wavelength was from 400 to 720 nm. The photocurrent response was measured using a Shanghai Chenhua CHI 660D workstation to evaluate the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency of the samples under visible light irradiation.

RhB was selected as the target pollutant to measure the photocatalytic activity. 10 mg of the sample were dispersed in 50 mL of RhB solution with a concentration of 10 mg L–1 and stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve adsorption saturation. After collecting 4 mL of the solution as the initial sample, the solution was irradiated under a 400 W metal halide lamp. 4 mL of the solution was collected at specified time intervals and then separated by centrifugation. To determine the stability of the sample, the solid obtained from centrifugal filtration was recovered, washed several times, and dried at 60 °C for 24 h, and then the degradation experiment was repeated. For the trapping experiments, 2.5 × 10–4 mol of KI, 2.5 × 10–4 mol of t-BuOH and 2.5 × 10–4 mol of CCl4 were used to trap the reactive species h+, e–, and ·OH, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process, structural and morphological analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the synthesis mechanism of the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. Firstly, g-C3N4 was obtained by high-temperature calcination of urea, and CDs were synthesized using urea and citric acid under hydrothermal conditions, respectively. Subsequently, the g-C3N4 and CDs are stirred and mixed well with the ferric chloride salt solution, and then heated. During the heating process, CDs are attracted to the g-C3N4 and loaded onto its surface by the advantages of electrostatic attraction and functional group binding [35,36]. Finally, 1D β-FeOOH nanorods were then loaded onto the CDs/g-C3N4 to form a CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. In order to have more confirmation of the successful construction of the composite, it was verified by several characterization methods.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples. The characteristic peak at 27.3° was indexed to the (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [1]. Diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 11.9 °, 16.9 °, 26.8 °, 34.1 °, 35.3 °, 44.6 °, 46.5, 52.2 °, 55.9 °, and 64.6 °, corresponding to the (110), (200), (310), (400), (211), (321), (200), (600), (521), and (541) crystal planes of β-FeOOH (JCPDS No. 75-1549), respectively [37]. Especially, the strongest peak from the (310) plane indicated that the β-FeOOH crystal grew along the c-axis which corresponds to the spindle or spinel-shaped orientation. Clearly, no peaks from other impurities can be detected for the CF, demonstrating the successful preparation of the g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. Meanwhile, the diffraction peak intensity of β-FeOOH decreased, suggesting the chemical interaction between β-FeOOH and g-C3N4. Obviously, a new peak at 24.4° appeared in the XRD patterns of the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites, which can be assigned to the characteristic peak of CDs [38,39]. Moreover, the characteristic peak of CDs became more evident as increasing the content of CDs for the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites, which indicated that the CDs were successfully loaded onto the composites.

In order to further characterize the changes with functional groups in the composites, the FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4, β-FeOOH, and CCF2 are depicted in Fig. 2(b). An absorption peak of β-FeOOH at 426.2 cm–1 was due to the tensile vibration of Fe-O, while the other two peaks at 690.4 and 846.6 cm–1 were ascribed to the contraction vibration of Fe-OH [40]. In addition, the peaks at 3430 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H stretching vibration in β-FeOOH [26]. In the case of pure g-C3N4, four absorption peaks at 806, 1329.8, 1422.8, and 1630.6 cm–1 belonged to the stretching vibration of the outer skeleton of triazine, the stretching vibration of the sp3 C-N bond in aromatic compounds, and the stretching vibration of sp2 C=N, respectively [41]. Moreover, the broad peak between 3500 and 3100 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H in g-C3N4 [42]. It is clear that all these peaks can be found in CCF2, indicating the presence of g-C3N4 and β-FeOOH components in composite photocatalysts. In addition, a weak new peak appeared at 2827 cm–1 in CCF2, likely owing to the coupling interaction between the β-FeOOH and the aromatic unit of g-C3N4 [28]. A similar phenomenon was also observed in previous studies [43]. The reason was ascribed to the formation of a heterojunction between β-FeOOH and g-C3N4 rather than physical adsorption [30].

The elemental composition, valence changes, and surface chemical states of each substance in CCF2 were analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3(a), the survey spectrum implied the presence of C, N, Fe, and O elements in CCF2. The C 1 s peaks (Fig. 3(b)) located at 284.6 and 288.5 eV were attributed to the surface coupling carbon of sp2-C and carbon in N-C = N of g-C3N4, respectively [44]. It should be noted that the peak of the CDs at 284.6 eV overlapped with the above peaks [45]. In addition, the peak at 285.3 eV represented the binding energy of C-O. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)), three peaks at 398.95, 399.55, and 400.95 eV were observed, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen, graphite nitrogen, and amino groups, respectively [46]. What's more, the Fe 2p spectrum can be deconvolved into two peaks centered at 711.6 and 724.85 eV, representing Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 in β-FeOOH, respectively [47]. Thus, XPS analysis confirmed the successful formation of CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites.

Fig. 4 illustrates the micromorphology of CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the size of FeOOH nanorods is relatively uniform by measurement, and their diameters are about 5 nm to 10 nm. Furthermore, the β-FeOOH with a length of approximately 1 μm was adhered to the surface of the layered g-C3N4. However, the CDs cannot be observed, mainly due to their low content and small size. Fig. 4(b) presents the TEM image of the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. It is obvious that the β-FeOOH was disorderly stacked on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets with a thickness of within nanometers. As exhibited in Fig. 4(c), the CDs with a particle size of approximately 5 nm were firmly anchored to the surface of the g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of 0.205 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of carbon [48]. In Fig. 4(d), the lattice spacing with 0.74 and 0.34 nm accorded with the (110) plane of β-FeOOH and the (002) plane of g-C3N4, respectively. The elemental mapping images were presented in Fig. 4(e, f), which clearly displayed the existence of C, N, Fe, and O elements. These results implied that the CDs were loaded onto the matrix and the CD/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite was successfully prepared, which were in agreement with those of XRD, FT-IR, and XPS.

The photocatalytic properties of the samples were analyzed by measuring the changes in the concentration of the RhB solution under visible light irradiation. Fig. 5 (a) shows the time dependence of RhB photodegradation over the as-synthesized samples. It is clear that the degradation rate of samples without CDs was 71 % after irradiation under visible light for 60 min, while the rates of CCF1, CCF2, and CCF3 were 92 %, 100 %, and 94 %, respectively. Obviously, the addition of CDs significantly improved the photocatalytic activity of the sample. The reason was mainly attributed to the upconversion effect of the CDs, which enhanced the light utilization rate of the sample and hindered the recombination of the photogenerated electron and hole pairs produced during the reaction [32]. To better understand the photodegradation dynamics, the photocatalytic reaction was considered to follow the first-order model [49]:
(1)
− ln
(
C t
/
C 0
)
= kt
where C 0 and Ct represent the initial concentration and the concentration at time t of the RhB solution, respectively. The reaction rate constants (k) can be obtained from the slope value of the fitting line in the plot of –ln(Ct /C 0) vs. t, and the results are displayed in Fig. 5(b). The k values of CF, CCF1, CCF2, and CCF3 were 0.027, 0.043, 0.057, and 0.046 min–1, respectively. Obviously, the k value of CCF2 was the highest among the samples, indicating that efficient ternary heterostructural contact is important for realizing excellent photocatalytic activity.

The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst are important factors for evaluating its practical application. Notably, the samples can be adsorbed out and transferred to the centrifuge tube by electromagnet after each experiment. It was washed by centrifugation several times and finally put into the oven to dry for the next cycle of the experiment. As shown in Fig. 5(c), after five cycles under uniform conditions, there was no apparent deactivation of the photocatalytic activity for CCF2. Meanwhile, the slightly decreasing photocatalytic activity was mainly owing to the mass loss of the samples during the process of recovery and filtration. Fig. 5(d) shows the XRD patterns of CCF2 before and after the cyclic reaction. There is no significant difference between the two patterns. The above results confirmed that the composite showed high stability under visible light irradiation due to the heterostructure.

To further explore the photocatalytic reaction mechanism, the optical characteristics of the samples were analyzed through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), pristine g-C3N4 displayed great light absorption ability in the ultraviolet region, which was consistent with previous reports. After combining with β-FeOOH, the optical response range of the composite was significantly broadened. With the introduction of CDs, the light absorption capacity of the visible region of the composite was further enhanced. This was mainly due to the increase in the number of reaction sites of the composite. Moreover, the up-conversion effect of the CDs improved the light utilization rate of the composite [50]. The following formula was used to estimate the band gap energy E g [51]:
(2)
α h ν =
( h ν −
E
g
)
n

The values of E g for g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/β-FeOOH were calculated to be 2.52 eV and 2.42 eV, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 6(b, c). By the same method, the band gap of CCF2 was calculated to be 2.28 eV (Fig. 6(d)).

The signal strength of the photocurrent represents the migration rate of photogenerated carriers of the photocatalyst. The higher the signal strength the higher the carrier separation and migration rates. Fig. 7 (a) shows the photocurrent-time response of g-C3N4, CF, and CCF2. The photocurrent of CCF2 was higher than those of g-C3N4 and CF, indicating the increasing yield of photogenerated electrons and holes in CCF2. This result showed that the introduction of CDs can accelerate the transfer and separation of photogenerated carriers, resulting in the enhanced catalytic activity of the sample. This originated from that the CDs have the special properties of electron transmission and electron storage.

The optical properties of the samples were analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(b). Generally, the strength of the PL emission signal directly corresponds to the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. The stronger the signal, the faster the recombination rate of charge carriers, which is not conducive to photocatalytic reactions [52]. According to Fig. 7(b), all the samples showed an emission peak centered at approximately 450 nm due to the g-C3N4 band-gap recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Clearly, CF exhibited the strongest peak, suggesting the fastest rate of electron-hole recombination. With the addition of CDs, the peak intensity of the samples decreased initially and reached the lowest value when the content of CDs was 6 %. After that, the peak intensity increased. These results confirmed that CCF2 had the highest yield of the photogenerated carriers among these samples.

To explain the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite and determine the main active substances involved in the catalytic process, a capture experiment of three active substances, namely, electrons, holes, and free radicals, was designed [53]. As shown in Fig. 8 , after adding t-BuOH into the reaction solution, the photocatalytic reaction became significantly slower. The result was caused by the capture of free radical ·OH, which led to the reduction of active substances in the reaction, thereby hindering the photocatalytic reaction. This result indicates that ·OH participated in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, after the addition of CCl4 and KI, the reaction was accelerated. This reason was that the added trapping agents captured e– and h+, which hindered the recombination of photogenerated carriers and promoted charge separation. According to the above result, the number of active substances h+ and e– in the photocatalytic reaction increased, promoting the photocatalytic reaction.

To obtain a better understanding of the carrier migration, the flat-band potential of CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite was analyzed using typical equations based on Mulliken electronegative theory [54–56]:
(3)
E CB
= X −
E
e
− 0.5
E
g
(4)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E
g
where the X values of g-C3N4 and β-FeOOH are 4.72 eV and 6.38 eV, respectively, which refer to their absolute electronegativity. As the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, E e is usually evaluated to be 4.5 eV. According to Eqs. (3) and (4), the E CB and E VB values of g-C3N4 were calculated to be –1.04 and 1.48 eV, respectively, and the corresponding E CB and E VB values of β-FeOOH were 1.01 and 2.76 eV.

In conclusion, it was achieved the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CD/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites and proposed a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism. According to their conduction band (CB) and valance band (VB) positions, g-C3N4 and β-FeOOH formed a heterojunction after combining. Under visible light irradiation, both g-C3N4 and β-FeOOH can be excited to generate photogenerated e– and h+. Since the conduction band position of g-C3N4 (–1.04) is more negative (1.01) than β-FeOOH, and the VB position of β-FeOOH is more positive than g-C3N4. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons generated from the excitation of g-C3N4 are transferred to the CB position of β-FeOOH by the built-in electric field, and the electrons interact with dissolved oxygen in water to generate superoxide radicals (-O2 –). However, the CB position of β-FeOOH is located at 1.01 eV, which does not meet the minimum conduction band potential for the generation of superoxide radicals (-O2 –) (E θ = (O2/-O2 –) = –0.33 eV). Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by the CD/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH complex does not conform to the type II charge transfer mechanism. Based on the above analysis, a more reasonable Z-Scheme charge transfer mechanism is proposed, as shown in Fig. 9 . Under visible light irradiation, β-FeOOH and g-C3N4 are excited to generate photo-generated e– and h+. and the e– is transferred from β-FeOOH VB to the CB position, and subsequently transferred from the CB position of β-FeOOH to the VB position of g-C3N4 under the effect of energy band bending and built-in electric field, and partially compounded with the holes generated from the VB of g-C3N4, while the e– generated from the excitation of g-C3N4 is transferred from its VB to CB and subsequently transferred rapidly to CDs to produce superoxide radicals (·O2 –) in the presence of oxygen, and can further interact with ·OOH to produce more oxidizing hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In addition, the holes left at the valence band of β-FeOOH are able to generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in the presence of h+ and water molecules. Therefore, the effective construction of the Z-Scheme charge transfer mechanism allows redox reactions to take place in the CB and VB bands of different substances, in which the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs can be effectively achieved and the strongest redox properties can be maintained. Notably, due to the addition of CDs, the number of active sites of the catalyst was increased, and the light absorption capacity was enhanced. Moreover, the up-conversion photoluminescence property of the CDs significantly increased the light utilization rate of the CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composite. Overall, the process of photocatalytic degradation reaction can be roughly divided into the following steps:
(5) g-C3N4/CDs/β-FeOOH + hν → e– + h+
(6) β-FeOOH + CB + e– → g-C3N4 + VB + e–
(7) g-C3N4 + VB + e– → g-C3N4 + CB + e–
(8) g-C3N4 + CB + e– + O2 → CDs+ CB + e– + O2
(9) CDs + e–+ O2 → ·O2 –
(10) ·O2 – + H2O → H2O2 + O2 + OH–
(11) H2O2 + e– → ·OH + OH–
(12) h+ + H2O →·OH + H+
(13) RhB + ·OH → Products

CDs/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites were fabricated by combining g-C3N4/β-FeOOH with the CDs, in which rod-shaped β-FeOOH of approximately 1 μm in length and CDs with a particle size of approximately 5 nm were deposited onto the surface of layered g-C3N4. When the content of CDs was 6 %, the photocatalytic performance of the composite was the best, and the degradation rate of RhB reached 100 % after 60 min under visible light. The light absorption capacity of the CD/g-C3N4/β-FeOOH composites was enhanced because the CDs provided more active sites. The construction of the Z-Scheme charge transfer mechanism effectively promotes the spatial separation of e– and h+ pairs, and the redox reactions occur in the conduction band and valence band of different substances, respectively, preserving their strongest redox properties. Moreover, due to the upconversion effect of the CDs, the light utilization rate of the composite was effectively improved. In addition, the catalytic dosimetry yield increased significantly after the addition of CDs, owing to the excellent electronic transmission capability of the CDs. Meanwhile, the sample still maintained good photocatalytic activity after five cycles of degradation. These satisfactory achievements provide a way for the reasonable design of high-performance photocatalyst.

Juhua Luo: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Xing Liu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jieliang Gu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Wenjie Zhao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Minming Gu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yu Xie: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21667019, 22066017).
